Laplace Transforms and Derivatives

Laplace Transforms and ODEs

Laplace Transforms and Systems of ODEs March 23, 2022 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Revision I Laplace transforms provide a mathematical tool to solve linear ODEs and systems of linear ODEs. In brief, the given ODE is transformed into an algebraic equation, which can be solved by purely algebraic manipulations. I Integration by parts The solution to this latter equation is then transformed back to yield the solution to the ODE. Z b Z b u dv = uv|ba − v du. I Applications are in many areas of engineering and physics, a a including signal processing, electrical networks, etc. I Definition of an improper integral: Note Z ∞ Z b Laplace transforms involve integrals, in particular improper f (t) dt = lim f (t) dt. integrals, which you should review in your own time. a b→∞ a Integration by parts and partial fractions are techniques frequently used in this topic. You should consolidate your skills on these techniques.

Rational functions Example 2 Find the partial fractions expansions of the following functions: I A function f is a rational function if it is of the form f (x) = P (x)/Q(x) where P (x) and Q(x) are 2x2 + 2x − 6 polynomials. 1. ; (x − 1)(x + 1)(x − 2) I A rational function f = P/Q is said to be proper if 8x + 9 2. . deg P < deg Q. (x − 2)(x + 3)2

I If a rational function f = P/Q is not proper, one can use long division to find polynomials p(x) and R(x) such that P (x) = p(x)Q(x) + R(x) with deg R < deg Q. Then f can be written as the sum of a polynomial and a proper rational function: P (x) R(x) f (x) = = p(x) + . Q(x) Q(x) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   2x2 + 2x − 6 1 1 2 (1) = − + (x − 1)(x + 1)(x − 2) x−1 x+1 x−2 8x + 9 1 1 3 (2) = − + (x − 2)(x + 3) 2 x − 2 x + 3 (x + 3)2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Laplace transforms

Definition 3 The Laplace transform of a function f : [0, ∞) → R is the function F defined by Z ∞ F (s) = e−st f (t) dt.

In this case, we also write F = Lf = L{f (t)}. Example 4 Find the Laplace transform F (s) if f (t) = 4t by using the definition. What is the domain of F ?

4/s2 , domain of F (s) = (0, ∞) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Existence of the Laplace transform Suppose that f (t) is defined for t > 0 and is locally integrable on [0, ∞), that is, Example 5 Z a |f (t)| dt < ∞ for any finite a > 0. Which of the following functions f (t) has a Laplace 0 transform F (s), and for which s? If, in addition, there are constants C > 0, b ≥ 0 and γ ≥ 0 1. f (t) = cos(1 + t2 ) such that cos(πt) |f (t)| ≤ Ceγt for t > b, 2. f (t) = t then the Laplace transform F (s) exists for s > γ, with e−2t 3. f (t) = Z b Z ∞ 1 + t4 −st |F (s)| ≤ |e f (t)| dt + |e−st f (t)| dt 4. f (t) = exp(3t2 ) 0 b Z b Z ∞ 5. f (t) = e5t log(1 + t) ≤ |f (t)| dt + Ce(γ−s)t dt 0 b Z b e−(s−γ)b = |f (t)| dt + C . 0 s−γ . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Remark The Laplace transform F (s) is a smooth function of s > γ even if f (t) is a discontinuous function of t. Example 6 Find the Laplace transform of the discontinuous function ( 3, 1 < t < 2, 0, otherwise.

Sketch the graphs of f (t) and F (s). Notice that the values of f (t) at any points of discontinuity have no effect on F (s).

How to evaluate the Laplace transform in practice?

I In practice we use a table of Laplace transforms, along with some transform theorems, to evaluate a given Laplace transform. One such table is provided in the tutorial problem booklet. It will also be provided in the final exam. I We will need the following definition, which extends the concept of factorial beyond the positive integers. Definition 9 (Gamma function) Z ∞ Γ(ν) = e−x xν−1 dx for ν > 0. 1 s a (1) for s > a (2) , for s > a s−a s2 − a2 s2 − a2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Example 12 Laplace transform is one-one Use the Table to find the Laplace transform of The Laplace transform of the zero function f (t) ≡ 0 is the √ f (t) = 3e7t − 6t e−2t + t. zero function F (s) ≡ 0. In fact, the next theorem shows that f (t) ≡ 0 is the only function whose Laplace transform is identically zero.

Theorem 13 Suppose that F (s) = L{f (t)}. If F (s) ≡ 0 then f (t) ≡ 0.

Now consider any two Laplace transforms F (s) = L{f (t)} and G(s) = L{g(t)}. Let h(t) = f (t) − g(t) then, by linearity, the Laplace transform H(s) = L{h(t)} = F (s) − G(s). If F (s) ≡ G(s), then the difference H(s) ≡ 0 and, by the Theorem, h(t) ≡ 0. Hence, f (t) ≡ g(t). In other words,

3 6 Γ(3/2) Lf = Lg implies f = g. − + s − 7 (s + 2)2 s3 /2 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Inverse Laplace transforms Linearity of the inverse transform Theorem 14 For any constants a and b, I Since the transformation L is one-one, it has an inverse transformation L−1 . That is, L−1 {aF (s) + bG(s)} = aL−1 F (s) + bL−1 G(s).

f (t) = L−1 {F (s)} iff F (s) = L{f (t)}. Proof. Let f (t) = L−1 {F (s)} and g(t) = L−1 {G(s)}. Since L is The notation f = L−1 F is justified because f (t) is linear, uniquely determined by F (s). aF (s) + bG(s) = aL{f (t)} + bL{g(t)} = L{af (t) + bg(t)} I There is an explicit formula for L−1 {F (s)} but it involves a contour integral in the complex plane. In practice, we and therefore just use the Laplace transform table backwards together with some interesting tricks. L−1 {aF (s) + bG(s)} = af (t) + bg(t) = aL−1 {F (s)} + bL−1 {G(s)}.

The Heaviside function u(t − a) Definition 16 The Heaviside (or unit step) function u(t) is defined by   0, t < 0, u(t) = 1/2, t = 0,   1, t > 0.

It follows that   0, t < a, u(t − a) = 1/2, t = a,   1, t > a.

The Heaviside function is useful for describing discontinuous (on-off) forces. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Effect of u(t − a)

Multiplying a function f (t) with u(t − a) produces all sorts of effects. For example, the product f (t)u(t − a) cuts the function f , whereas f (t − a)u(t − a) shifts and cuts.

Example 21 Let f (t) = t2 . Sketch each of the following functions. 1. g(t) = f (t)u(t − 4) (This is a cut.) 2. g(t) = f (t − 4)u(t − 4) (This is a shift plus cut.)  3. g(t) = f (t) u(t − 2) − u(t − 7) .  −3s  e e−4s 7 − s s . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

The shifting theorems Proof

The next two theorems are essential when dealing with inverse Laplace transforms. Let L{f (t)} = F (s). Then Theorem 23 (First Shifting Theorem) Z ∞ −at Suppose that L{f (t)} = F (s). Then L{e f (t)} = e−st e−at f (t) dt Z0 ∞ −at L{e f (t)} = F (s + a) = e−(s+a)t f (t) dt (a shift in s) and hence = F (s + a) .

L−1 {F (s + a)} = e−at f (t).
